And the first one, which is a new mode that we have in Claude Code, which is the auto mode. Auto mode is that, for that specific case, when you are working, put a prompt into Claude and think, hey, okay, this is going to run for some time, and come back, is that an authorization request. Hey, can I run this? Can I read this file, for example? Do you authorize me to access this folder? So, that was quite frustrating, and with auto mode, those days are gone. What auto mode is doing is first leveraging the high capability of the latest models as well to assess if a situation is destructive or not, because what auto mode does is that for every time where otherwise Claude will stop and go to you and say, hey, can I get permission for this bit? Auto mode checks that with a classifier that checks two things. So, the first thing is, is this action destructive? Am I going to regret this down the line? So, yeah, that's the first check. The second check is pretty much, does this look like a prompt injection? So, yeah, is this part of the situation that looks like that is going to inject something on my prompt? So, if the checks passes, so none of this is true, Claude takes the action on your behalf. So, it doesn't stop and it continues running. If any of those checks fails, what happens is that Claude will try to find a workaround, maybe a scenario, there's another path that we don't have to go and ask for those particular permissions and don't have that destructive way that we are going. And if that case is not as well possible, then Claude will come to you and say, hey, I need your permission to execute this bit. So, this gives Claude a lot more autonomy and don't bother you when you don't need to be. So, for example, we value a lot of your time and Claude should earn your time with that as well. The idea here is that a work tree is a copy of your project in a segregated subdirectory that is only assessed by one Claude session. Actually, work trees existed way before Claude code, and they are supported by Claude code since the early beginnings. But to be honest, it was quite tricky to get it right because you have to manage all these different directories. Work trees are kind of ephemeral if you start working on something and stop it. So managing all of that yourself was something that was quite tricky. But now it's super simple because Claude code now has this native support for work trees. Whenever you want to start working in a new feature that needs to be parallelized amongst several sessions, you can just start your session using Claude dash dash work tree, and Claude will copy that repo to a segregated directory and work on it independently.
